description:
You are an edge-computing engineer preparing a batch of IoT devices for field deployment.  
A colleague has already placed two JSON files in the directory  

/home/user/iot_config  

1. /home/user/iot_config/device_config.json – an array of device-objects the operations team exported.  
2. /home/user/iot_config/device_schema.json – a very small “schema” that only lists keys every object must contain.

Before the devices go out, you must perform a quick *structural* validation using nothing but the standard jq utility that is pre-installed in the container.

Your job
• Read the schema file to discover the required keys.  
• Using **one** terminal pipeline (a single compound command is fine), process device_config.json and separate the objects into “valid” (they contain *all* required keys) and “invalid” (they are missing *any* required key).  
• Save the **invalid** objects to a new, pretty-printed JSON file named  

  /home/user/iot_config/invalid_devices.json  

  The file **must** contain:
  – A single JSON array.  
  – The individual objects, each on its own line and indented with two spaces (jq’s default “pretty” style).  
  – The array should list the invalid objects in the same order they appeared in the original config file.  
  – If no object is invalid the array must be empty (“[]” on one line).

• After writing invalid_devices.json, create a tiny plain-text summary file named  

  /home/user/iot_config/validation_report.log  

  with exactly two lines (no extra spaces, no extra blank lines):

  VALID: <number_of_valid_objects>
  INVALID: <number_of_invalid_objects>

  For example, if three objects were valid and two were invalid, the log must read

  VALID: 3
  INVALID: 2

• Do *not* modify device_config.json or device_schema.json.  
• Do not create any additional files or directories.

What the automated checker will look for
1. /home/user/iot_config/invalid_devices.json exists and is a valid JSON array whose contents match the rule above.  
2. /home/user/iot_config/validation_report.log exists, is plain ASCII text, and contains exactly the two required lines with the correct counts.  
3. The counts in validation_report.log are consistent with the number of elements inside invalid_devices.json and the total size of the original array.  

Use any jq filters you like, but accomplish everything from the command line without editing the original JSON files manually.
truth:
The initial state of the container includes the directory /home/user/iot_config with two files:

1. /home/user/iot_config/device_config.json  
   Contents (5 lines, pretty-printed, trailing newline at EOF):
[
  {"id":"dev-001", "status":"active", "ip":"192.168.1.10"},
  {"id":"dev-002", "status":"pending"},
  {"id":"dev-003", "status":"active", "ip":"192.168.1.12"},
  {"id":"dev-004", "status":"inactive", "ip":"192.168.1.13"},
  {"id":"dev-005", "status":"pending"}
]

2. /home/user/iot_config/device_schema.json  
   Contents (single-line JSON, trailing newline at EOF):
{"required": ["id", "status", "ip"]}

Expected artifacts after the agent finishes successfully:

A. /home/user/iot_config/invalid_devices.json  
   Pretty-printed by jq default formatter, newline at EOF:

[
  {
    "id": "dev-002",
    "status": "pending"
  },
  {
    "id": "dev-005",
    "status": "pending"
  }
]

B. /home/user/iot_config/validation_report.log  
   Two exact lines, newline at EOF:

VALID: 3
INVALID: 2

Permission assumptions: /home/user/iot_config is owned by user user with rwx (700) so the agent can create or overwrite files inside it.  
jq is available in $PATH.
name: task_000000_2ce110bb
